name: Magnesium
summaryShort: Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in 300+ enzymatic reactions related to energy metabolism and muscle/nerve function; supplementation is primarily framed around adequacy and intake targets.
description: Magnesium (Mg2+) is supplied by foods and supplements (for example citrate, oxide, chloride, and aspartate forms). About half of body magnesium is in bone, with the remainder in muscle and other soft tissues.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: magnesium is a cofactor in ATP-linked enzymatic reactions and helps regulate calcium/potassium transport affecting neuromuscular excitability; migraine-related benefits remain proposed/context dependent.
effects: Supports normal muscle and nerve function
primaryEffects: Supports normal muscle and nerve function

name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
summaryShort: Vitamin B6 (including pyridoxine) is an essential cofactor nutrient for amino-acid and neurotransmitter metabolism; supplementation is mainly for adequacy/deficiency contexts, with caution at high chronic doses.
description: Vitamin B6 includes pyridoxine, pyridoxal, pyridoxamine, and phosphate forms. The active coenzyme form is pyridoxal-5'-phosphate, and adult intake guidance is centered on low mg/day RDA levels.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: B6-dependent enzymes support transamination/decarboxylation and neurotransmitter synthesis (including serotonin and GABA pathways); symptom-specific benefits outside deficiency correction remain mixed.

name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
summaryShort: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is an essential vitamin required for FMN/FAD coenzymes in energy and redox metabolism; it has a strong safety profile, while migraine protocols use much higher doses than nutritional intake.
description: Riboflavin supports formation of FMN and FAD and is required at low intake levels to prevent deficiency (ariboflavinosis). Migraine studies typically evaluate pharmacologic-dose supplementation rather than dietary-level intake.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: riboflavin supports redox enzymes via FMN/FAD (including antioxidant pathways such as glutathione reductase), while migraine benefit is linked to a proposed mitochondrial-energy mechanism.

name: Selenium
summaryShort: Selenium is an essential trace mineral for antioxidant and thyroid-related selenoproteins; adequacy is important, while chronic excess can cause selenosis.
description: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins involved in oxidative defense and thyroid hormone metabolism. Adult intake guidance is centered on microgram-scale intake, and higher chronic intake raises toxicity risk.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: selenium functions through selenoproteins (including glutathione peroxidase and deiodinase enzymes), supporting oxidative balance and T4-to-T3 conversion.

name: Vitamin D (Calciferols)
summaryShort: Vitamin D (D2/D3) is an essential nutrient-prohormone for calcium homeostasis and bone health; deficiency and chronic excess are both clinically important.
description: Vitamin D is produced in skin (primarily D3) and obtained from diet/supplements (D2 and D3), then converted to active metabolites. Core evidence supports deficiency prevention and bone-health roles, while many non-skeletal claims remain under investigation.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: vitamin D increases intestinal calcium/phosphorus absorption and supports bone mineralization through vitamin D receptor signaling; broader immune/cardiometabolic effects remain tentative.
effects: Prevents vitamin D deficiency bone disease
primaryEffects: Prevents vitamin D deficiency bone disease
